summary: The notebook introduces and operationalizes a custom defensive metric called Air-Time Defense Closure (ATDC), which quantifies how quickly defenders close the gap to receivers during a pass. It demonstrates the metric's calculation using synthetically generated tracking data, visualizes its distribution and spatial-temporal relationships, and builds baseline regression models to predict defensive effectiveness. The author shows that features like initial separation and speed differentials are strong predictors of ATDC, providing a reproducible framework for evaluating defensive coverage in NFL passing plays. Readers should understand how to translate spatial-temporal tracking data into actionable performance metrics and validate them with simple machine learning baselines.
competition_flow: Generates synthetic NFL tracking data, calculates a custom Air-Time Defense Closure metric, visualizes spatial-temporal relationships, engineers predictive features, and trains baseline regression models to evaluate defensive coverage effectiveness.
data_processing: Categorical binning of ATDC scores into performance tiers
features_engineering: Speed differential between defender and receiver
Pursuit efficiency (closure distance normalized by air time and defender speed)
Binned ATDC performance categories (Poor, Average, Good, Excellent)
ensembling: None. The notebook trains and compares individual models without combining them.
insights: ATDC effectively quantifies defensive coverage by combining initial separation, final separation, and ball flight time.
Defender-receiver speed differential and initial distance are the strongest predictors of defensive closure success.
Tree-based models capture non-linear relationships in tracking data better than linear regression.
Cross-validation confirms that baseline regression models generalize reasonably well to unseen synthetic plays.
